title: Netherlands SyRI welfare-fraud risk-indication system struck down
primary_source_id: District Court of The Hague | Judgment 5 Feb 2020, ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:865
paper_use_note: A Dutch court ruled the SyRI welfare-fraud detection legislation breached Article 8 ECHR and ordered the system stopped.
verification_gap: First-instance ruling on legislative proportionality, not a technical audit of model bias.

title: Netherlands childcare-benefits (toeslagenaffaire) discriminatory risk-profiling fine
primary_source_id: Dutch DPA (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) | EUR 2.75M fine, 7 Dec 2021
ai_system_type: self-learning fraud risk classifier using nationality
paper_use_note: The Dutch DPA fined the Tax Administration EUR 2.75M for unlawfully and discriminatorily using nationality data to flag childcare-benefit fraud; the scandal forced the cabinet's 2021 resignation.
verification_gap: DPA finding on data-protection grounds; the ~26,000 wrongful-accusation figure is a government estimate, not from the fine decision.

title: Netherlands FSV fraud-blacklist fine
primary_source_id: Dutch DPA (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) | EUR 3.7M fine, 7 Apr 2022
paper_use_note: The Dutch DPA imposed a EUR 3.7M fine on the Tax Administration for six GDPR violations in operating the FSV blacklist, holding data on ~270,000 people.
verification_gap: Distinct system/fine from the EUR 2.75M childcare-nationality fine; FSV is a blacklist rather than an ML model.

record_status: Royal Commission; Federal Court of Austr
title: Australia Robodebt automated debt-recovery scheme ruled unlawful
primary_source_id: Royal Commission; Federal Court of Australia | Royal Commission final report 7 July 2023; Prygodicz v Commonwealth (No 2) [2021] FCA 634
paper_use_note: An Australian Royal Commission found Robodebt unlawful and 'crude and cruel'; a related Federal Court class action settled for AUD 112M plus debt cancellation.
verification_gap: Some referral/prosecution outcomes sealed/ongoing; the scheme used algorithmic income-averaging rather than a learned ML model.

title: UK Ofqual 2020 A-level/GCSE grade-standardisation algorithm withdrawn
primary_source_id: Office for Statistics Regulation; Ofqual | Algorithm withdrawn 17 Aug 2020; OSR review report 2 Mar 2021
paper_use_note: Ofqual's 2020 grade-standardisation algorithm downgraded ~40% of teacher-predicted A-level grades with disproportionate impact on state-school pupils, was withdrawn within days, and was criticised in an official OSR review.
verification_gap: Official anchor is a regulator process review, not a court ruling; withdrawal was a policy U-turn.

record_status: Michigan Supreme Court; Court of Claims
title: Michigan MiDAS unemployment false-fraud automated system
primary_source_id: Michigan Supreme Court; Court of Claims | Bauserman v. UIA, 26 July 2022; USD 20M settlement Jan 2024
paper_use_note: Michigan's MiDAS auto-flagged tens of thousands as fraudulent (a state audit found ~93% of reviewed determinations wrong); the Supreme Court allowed a due-process class action that settled for USD 20M.
verification_gap: Ruling allowed the claim to proceed, not a final merits adjudication; 93% figure from a 2016 sample audit.

title: France CNAF welfare risk-scoring algorithm legal challenge
primary_source_id: Conseil d'Etat (pending); Defenseur des Droits | Challenge filed Oct 2024; Ombudsperson opinion Oct 2024
paper_use_note: Civil-society organisations are challenging the CNAF welfare risk-scoring algorithm before the Conseil d'Etat as discriminatory, with a supporting Ombudsperson opinion.
verification_gap: No final ruling yet; strongest record is the Ombudsperson opinion and admitted challenge.

title: Poland unemployment-profiling scoring system ruled unconstitutional
primary_source_id: Constitutional Tribunal | Judgment K 53/16, 6 June 2018; system scrapped by Dec 2019
paper_use_note: Poland's Constitutional Tribunal held the unemployment-profiling scheme unconstitutional because key parameters were set by ministerial ordinance rather than statute; the system was scrapped.
verification_gap: Ruling rested on legality/separation-of-powers, not a finding of algorithmic discrimination.

title: Sweden Trelleborg automated welfare-decision source-code disclosure ruling
primary_source_id: Swedish Administrative Court | Administrative-court FOI ruling ~2019-2020 (source code is a public document)
paper_use_note: After FOI litigation a Swedish administrative court held that Trelleborg's automated social-assistance decision system's source code was an official public document subject to disclosure.
verification_gap: Transparency/FOI ruling, not a finding the system itself was unlawful; exact court/date not pinned to a primary citation.

title: UK Home Office visa-application 'streaming tool' algorithm withdrawn
primary_source_id: High Court judicial review (JCWI/Foxglove); Home Office | Home Office agreed to discontinue the tool, 3-4 Aug 2020
paper_use_note: Facing a judicial review alleging racial discrimination under the Equality Act, the Home Office committed in Aug 2020 to suspend and redesign its visa-streaming algorithm.
verification_gap: No merits judgment; withdrawn pre-hearing, so unlawfulness was alleged not adjudicated.

record_status: U.S. District Court D. Idaho; 9th Circui
title: Idaho Medicaid benefit-cut algorithm (K.W. v. Armstrong)
primary_source_id: U.S. District Court D. Idaho; 9th Circuit | 9th Cir. 5 June 2015; D. Idaho due-process decision 29 Mar 2016
paper_use_note: A US federal court held Idaho's secret budget-calculation formula for Medicaid disability assistance arbitrarily deprived participants of property without due process and ordered it disclosed with safeguards.
verification_gap: Tool was a statistical formula not a learned ML model; rulings turned on due-process/transparency, not bias.

title: Houston (HISD) EVAAS teacher value-added evaluation algorithm
primary_source_id: U.S. District Court S.D. Texas | Houston Fed'n of Teachers v. HISD, 251 F.Supp.3d 1168 (S.D. Tex. 2017); settled Oct 2017
paper_use_note: A US federal court held that using secret, unverifiable EVAAS value-added scores to terminate Houston teachers raised a viable procedural-due-process claim; the district then abandoned the practice in settlement.
verification_gap: Denial of summary judgment (claim survived), not a final merits finding; resolved by settlement.

title: Spain BOSCO energy-subsidy eligibility algorithm transparency case
primary_source_id: Tribunal Supremo; Audiencia Nacional | Supreme Court ruling Sept 2025 ordering source-code release, reversing Audiencia Nacional 30 Apr 2024
ai_system_type: eligibility-determination algorithm for the electricity social bonus
paper_use_note: After BOSCO wrongly denied energy-bill subsidies, Spain's Supreme Court ruled in 2025 that the government must disclose the algorithm's source code, a public-algorithm transparency precedent.
verification_gap: Concerns transparency/source-code access, not a finding that BOSCO's outputs were unlawful; exact Sept 2025 day varies across reports.

title: Uber ATG self-driving fatality (Elaine Herzberg, Tempe AZ)
primary_source_id: NTSB; Maricopa County Superior Court | NTSB report HAR-19/03 adopted 19 Nov 2019; safety driver guilty plea 28 July 2023
paper_use_note: NTSB found the automated driving system failed to classify the pedestrian and brake while the inattentive safety driver failed to intervene, in the first pedestrian fatality involving a self-driving test vehicle.
verification_gap: NTSB assigned primary cause to the safety driver's distraction and Uber's safety culture; the corporation was not criminally charged.

record_status: California DMV; CPUC; NHTSA; U.S. DOJ
title: Cruise (GM) robotaxi pedestrian-dragging incident, San Francisco
primary_source_id: California DMV; CPUC; NHTSA; U.S. DOJ | Incident 2 Oct 2023; DMV permit suspension 24 Oct 2023; NHTSA $1.5M penalty 30 Sept 2024; DOJ $500K fine
paper_use_note: A Cruise driverless vehicle dragged a pedestrian ~20 feet after a secondary maneuver, and the company later admitted omitting the dragging from regulator reports, drawing multiple penalties.
verification_gap: The pedestrian was first struck by a separate human-driven car; penalties focus heavily on Cruise's false reporting.

title: Tesla Autopilot fatal-crash civil verdict (Benavides v. Tesla, FL)
primary_source_id: U.S. District Court, S.D. Florida (federal jury) | Verdict 1 Aug 2025 finding Tesla 33% liable, ~$243M; verdict upheld Feb 2026, on appeal
paper_use_note: A federal jury found Tesla's Autopilot design partly responsible for a fatal crash, the first US wrongful-death verdict tying liability to Autopilot operation.
verification_gap: Apportioned only 33% fault to Tesla (driver primarily at fault); Tesla is appealing, so liability is contested.

title: Tesla Autopilot/Autosteer NHTSA recall (2M+ vehicles)
primary_source_id: NHTSA | Recall 23V-838 announced 13 Dec 2023 (~2.03M vehicles)
paper_use_note: NHTSA determined Autopilot's driver-monitoring safeguards were insufficient to prevent foreseeable misuse, forcing Tesla's largest-ever recall and an over-the-air fix.
verification_gap: Remedy was a software/warning update; NHTSA kept the investigation open to assess efficacy.

title: Tesla Full Self-Driving (FSD) reduced-visibility NHTSA investigation
primary_source_id: NHTSA Office of Defects Investigation | Preliminary Evaluation opened 17 Oct 2024 (incl. 1 pedestrian fatality); upgraded to Engineering Analysis Mar 2026
paper_use_note: NHTSA is investigating Tesla FSD crashes in low-visibility conditions including a pedestrian death, citing camera-degradation detection/warning failures.
verification_gap: Open Engineering Analysis; no recall or final defect finding yet.

title: Tesla 'phantom braking' NHTSA investigation
primary_source_id: NHTSA Office of Defects Investigation | Preliminary Evaluation PE22-002 opened Feb 2022 (~416,000 vehicles)
paper_use_note: NHTSA opened a formal probe into unexpected, unwarranted automatic braking by Tesla driver-assistance systems at highway speeds.
verification_gap: Complaint-driven preliminary evaluation; no confirmed fatalities, recall, or final defect determination.

title: Waymo 5th-gen ADS barrier-collision recall
primary_source_id: NHTSA | Recall 25E-034 (May 2025), 1,212 vehicles; followed a 2024 preliminary evaluation
paper_use_note: Waymo recalled software after its driverless vehicles repeatedly collided with stationary/semi-stationary roadway barriers under an open NHTSA investigation.
verification_gap: No injuries reported; a software fix was deployed before the formal recall (property-damage/defect case).

title: South Korea industrial robot crushes inspection worker
primary_source_id: South Gyeongsang Provincial Police investigation | Incident 7 Nov 2023 (robot misidentified worker as a box)
paper_use_note: A palletizing robot apparently failed to distinguish a human worker from the produce boxes it was handling and fatally crushed him during a sensor inspection.
verification_gap: Early police evidence pointed toward human error/procedure as much as robot defect; no final determination reflected.

record_status: New York State Department of Financial S
title: Apple Card / Goldman Sachs gender-bias credit-limit investigation
primary_source_id: New York State Department of Financial Services (DFS) | DFS investigation report, 23 Mar 2021
paper_use_note: After viral gender-bias allegations, NY DFS found no evidence of unlawful sex-based discrimination in Apple Card underwriting but criticized transparency and customer-service deficiencies.
verification_gap: DFS cleared the program, so this is a documented non-finding of discrimination rather than a confirmed bias case.

title: State v. Loomis (COMPAS risk-assessment at sentencing)
primary_source_id: Supreme Court of Wisconsin | 2016 WI 68, decided 13 July 2016
paper_use_note: The Wisconsin Supreme Court upheld use of COMPAS at sentencing against a due-process challenge but imposed limits and required cautionary warnings about its proprietary, group-based nature.
verification_gap: Ruling permitted the tool rather than finding it biased; ProPublica-style bias claims were not adjudicated as fact.

record_status: Arkansas Supreme Court; Pulaski County C
title: Ark. Dep't of Human Servs. v. Ledgerwood (Arkansas Medicaid ARChoices algorithm)
primary_source_id: Arkansas Supreme Court; Pulaski County Circuit Court | 530 S.W.3d 336 (2017); rule invalidated/enjoined 2018
paper_use_note: Arkansas state courts invalidated/enjoined the algorithmic ARChoices hours-allocation rule for failing rulemaking/notice requirements, after cuts averaging ~43% of attendant-care hours.
verification_gap: Decided on administrative-procedure/notice grounds, not a holding that the algorithm was statistically biased.

title: CFPB action against Hello Digit (faulty automated savings algorithm)
primary_source_id: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) | Consent order 10 Aug 2022; $2.7M penalty
paper_use_note: CFPB found Digit's automated savings algorithm deceptively caused consumer overdrafts despite a no-overdraft guarantee and kept interest, imposing a $2.7M penalty plus restitution.
verification_gap: UDAAP/deception enforcement over a faulty algorithm, not a discrimination/fair-lending finding.

record_status: General Court of the EU / CJEU
title: iBorderCtrl AI 'lie detector' transparency ruling
primary_source_id: General Court of the EU / CJEU | T-158/19, 15 Dec 2021; upheld C-135/22 P, 7 Sept 2023
paper_use_note: The EU courts ordered partial disclosure of documents on the ethics and legality of the iBorderCtrl AI deception-detection research, recognizing a public interest in oversight of such surveillance tech.
verification_gap: A freedom-of-information/transparency decision about a research project, not a finding the system was discriminatory or deployed at scale.

record_status: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Ca
title: OPC Canada joint finding: Clearview AI scraping violated PIPEDA
primary_source_id: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (+ QC/BC/AB) | PIPEDA Findings #2021-001, 2 Feb 2021
paper_use_note: Four Canadian privacy regulators jointly concluded Clearview's mass scraping and biometric templating of faces without consent breached PIPEDA and provincial laws.
verification_gap: Finding with recommendations rather than a fine; OPC lacked direct order-making power at the time and Clearview contested applicability.

record_status: Office of the Australian Information Com
title: OAIC Australia determination: Clearview AI breached the Privacy Act 1988
primary_source_id: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner | Commissioner-initiated determination, 14 Oct 2021
paper_use_note: OAIC formally determined Clearview breached several Australian Privacy Principles and ordered it to cease collection and destroy Australians' images and biometric templates.
verification_gap: OAIC discontinued further enforcement against Clearview in Aug 2024, leaving destruction-order compliance unverified.

title: Beijing Internet Court: AI-generated image qualifies for copyright protection
primary_source_id: Beijing Internet Court | Li v. Liu judgment, 27 Nov 2023
paper_use_note: The court held a Stable-Diffusion image reflecting the user's prompt selection and parameter tuning met the originality threshold and was copyrightable, ordering apology plus RMB 500.
verification_gap: First-instance Internet Court ruling on specific facts; not binding nationwide precedent.

title: Beijing Internet Court: unauthorized AI voice cloning infringes personality rights
primary_source_id: Beijing Internet Court | Yin v. defendants judgment, 23 Apr 2024
paper_use_note: China's first AI-voice case held that training a voice generator on a dubber's recordings and selling the synthetic voice without consent infringed her personality rights, awarding RMB 250,000.
verification_gap: First-instance judgment; downstream defendants who unknowingly used the product escaped liability.

title: Guangzhou Internet Court: generative-AI provider liable for infringing Ultraman outputs
primary_source_id: Guangzhou Internet Court | SCLA v. AIGC platform judgment, Feb 2024
paper_use_note: In a first AIGC-platform infringement ruling, the court found an AI image service liable when prompts produced Ultraman-like images, awarding RMB 10,000 and imposing duty-of-care obligations.
verification_gap: First-instance ruling against one platform; the 'reasonable care' duty scope is untested on appeal.

title: Beijing Internet Court: AI face-swapping app infringed personal-information rights
primary_source_id: Beijing Internet Court | Models v. face-swap app operator judgment, 20 June 2024
paper_use_note: The court held that using individuals' videos to build paid face-swap templates without consent infringed their personal-information rights.
verification_gap: First-instance ruling drawing a fine portrait-rights vs personal-information-rights distinction; not appellate.

title: PPC Japan administrative guidance to OpenAI on ChatGPT data handling
primary_source_id: Personal Information Protection Commission (PPC) | Administrative guidance to OpenAI, June 2023
ai_system_type: large language model / generative AI chatbot
paper_use_note: Japan's PPC issued formal administrative guidance directing OpenAI not to collect sensitive personal data for training without consent and to minimize collection.
verification_gap: Administrative guidance is non-binding (no penalty/order).

title: PDPC Singapore: AI/algorithmic evaluative outputs within Access Obligation exception
primary_source_id: Personal Data Protection Commission | [Applicant] v. HSBC Bank (Singapore), [2021] SGPDPC 3
paper_use_note: The PDPC ruled a bank could withhold opinion data generated by AI/algorithmic systems in a declined credit-card decision under the PDPA's evaluative-purpose exception.
verification_gap: Decision favored the organisation and concerned access rights, not a finding of AI harm or a penalty.

title: ASIC Australia: former Metigy CEO pleaded guilty over false 'AI' company claims
primary_source_id: ASIC / Commonwealth DPP | Charges 18 Nov 2024 (24-248MR); guilty plea Sept 2025 (25-263MR)
paper_use_note: The CEO of AI-marketing startup Metigy pleaded guilty to making false/misleading statements to investors (revenue ~AUD 43,000 vs claims backing ~AUD 39M raised).
verification_gap: Guilty plea to misleading-statements/dishonesty broadly; sentencing pending and charges centre on financials. | VERIFIED: charge/first appearance 8 Nov 2024; guilty plea announced 4 Nov 2025 (anchor dates off).

title: Delhi High Court: John Doe injunction protecting Anil Kapoor against AI deepfakes
primary_source_id: High Court of Delhi | Anil Kapoor v. Simply Life India, interim order 20 Sept 2023
ai_system_type: generative AI deepfakes / face-morphing / voice cloning
paper_use_note: The Delhi High Court granted an omnibus injunction restraining entities from using AI, face-morphing or voice tools to exploit the actor's name, likeness and voice for commercial gain.
verification_gap: Interim/interlocutory protective order granted ex parte, not a final merits judgment.

title: Colombian Constitutional Court T-323/24 reviews judicial ChatGPT use and due-process criteria
primary_source_id: Corte Constitucional de Colombia | Sentencia T-323 de 2024, 2024-08-02
ai_system_type: generative AI used in judicial drafting
paper_use_note: Official judgment T-323/24 reviewed a tutela matter where the second-instance judge used ChatGPT 3.5 and discussed due-process criteria for judicial use of AI tools.
verification_gap: Claim ceiling: do not state that AI decided the case, that AI use alone violated due process, or that the ruling proves prevalence or legality/illegality of AI use in Colombian courts.

record_status: Government of Kenya / High Court of Keny
title: Kenya: government suspension and High Court ruling against Worldcoin iris collection
primary_source_id: Government of Kenya / High Court of Kenya | Govt suspension Aug 2023; High Court ruling 5 May 2025
paper_use_note: After suspending Worldcoin in 2023, the Kenya High Court ruled its iris-biometric collection unlawful under Kenyan data-protection law and ordered deletion of all collected biometric data.
verification_gap: 2023 step was an executive suspension; binding illegality finding came via the 2025 court ruling.

record_status: Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados
title: ANPD Brazil: suspension of Worldcoin biometric-for-crypto collection
primary_source_id: Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados (ANPD) | Preventive suspension Jan 2025; appeal rejected Feb 2025
paper_use_note: ANPD ordered Tools for Humanity to stop offering crypto compensation for iris/biometric data, holding financial incentives could vitiate free, informed consent, with daily fines for non-compliance.
verification_gap: Preventive/administrative measure (upheld on appeal) rather than a final adjudicated penalty.
